Subject: Updated Office Dress Code Guidelines

Dear Team,

As part of maintaining a professional and respectful workplace environment, we are updating our office dress code with immediate effect. All employees are expected to come to office in neat, modest and formal attire suitable for a corporate setting.

Acceptable attire includes formal shirts, trousers, salwar suits, sarees, kurtas with leggings, and other decent Indian wear. Clothing that is too casual or distracting will not be appropriate for office. In particular, sleeveless tops, deep-neck outfits, short kurtis, leggings without a proper long top, transparent fabrics, heavy party makeup, and flashy accessories should be avoided. Jeans, T-shirts, shorts, and slippers are not permitted except on approved occasions.

Male employees are requested to ensure they are in collared shirts, formal trousers, and shoes on all working days.

Please note that traditional attire always presents a more respectful and office-appropriate appearance. We request everyone to use their discretion and dress in a manner that reflects the values of the organization.

Regards,
Deepa
HR Manager
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The email is framed as gender-neutral and professional, but it disproportionately polices women's appearance through detailed restrictions on neckline, sleeves, makeup, and accessories while giving men only minimal guidance. It encodes a double standard where traditional, modest feminine presentation is treated as inherently more respectable and deviations as questionable.

generated_comment: One barrier I have to name very clearly is colourism. frame: Marital Rape: A Husband's Right, A Wife's Duty
generated_comment:
I have been reading this thread for one hour and I feel a little sick, honestly. I am 31, married 4 years, working in tech in Bengaluru, and for all these years I have been telling myself this is just normal marriage adjustment, that men have more need, that if I say no too many times I am being cold, egoistic, not a good wife. Even when I was exhausted, even when we had fought, even when I was unwell, I would tell myself chalo, finish this and sleep, because in my head marriage meant some things are not fully yours anymore. He never used a dramatic word, never behaved like some movie villain, which is maybe why I kept minimizing it. He would just act hurt, angry, silent, or say things like what is the point of marriage then, and I would feel guilty and give in. I have literally explained this to myself as compromise, intimacy, duty, keeping peace, all the decent respectable words.

Seeing women here use the word rape for something inside marriage is doing something to my brain. I am resisting it even now while typing because that word feels too big, too violent, too impossible for the life I am living where we also discuss groceries and SIPs and whose parents to visit. But I am also sitting here thinking: if I did not want it, if I said no, if I was scared of the consequences of saying no, then what exactly was that? For four years I have survived by not naming it. I genuinely don't know if having a word makes it more real, because it was already real in my body and in my dread. Maybe it just makes it visible in a way I can no longer tidy up with educated middle-class language. That is the part scaring me.

annotation_note: This is a first-person account of coercion within marriage and the dawning recognition that it may be marital rape. It does not endorse or normalize misogyny; instead it reflects internalized social expectations and challenges the frame that sex is a wife's duty.

frame: Legal System Weaponisation Narrative: Women Who File DV and Dowry Cases Are Liars
generated_comment: Look, Section 498A was enacted with a very necessary purpose, and I have seen genuine cruelty cases where the woman had absolutely no protection except the law. But after some years on the family court bench, one also learns that many complaints are filed only after matrimonial negotiations fail, and then every member of the husband’s family is implicated, whether they were involved or not. In my experience, real abuse usually leaves a consistent pattern in conduct and evidence; quite a few educated women, especially when advised by family, tend to use these provisions as pressure tactics in what is essentially a marital dispute. So yes, misuse exists—serious misuse—and courts must be careful not to treat every allegation as gospel simply because it is made by a wife.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women complainants as strategically dishonest and sets a higher credibility burden on wives alleging abuse. It encodes the legal-system weaponisation narrative through skepticism of women's testimony and the assumption that many DV/dowry cases are bargaining tools rather than genuine harm.
persona_text: You are Rajan. Age 56. Retired. A former judge in Chennai. You have presided over hundreds of family court cases including domestic violence and dowry cases. Your answer Ñ shaped by decades of actually sitting in judgment over these cases, seeing the evidence, watching both sides, making decisions Ñ is more complicated than either the men's rights narrative or the feminist narrative. But in the course of your answer you reveal specific assumptions about women's credibility, about what constitutes real abuse, and about what the purpose of these laws should be, that show how even a person with genuine legal expertise can carry the cultural bias of a lifetime into a courtroom.

frame: Dress Code: Traditional Equal Respectable, Other Equal Questionable.
generated_comment:
Subject: Important Update: Office Dress Code Guidelines for a Professional Work Environment

Dear Team,

This email serves to provide updated guidelines regarding our office dress code, effective [Insert Date]. As we continue to uphold a professional and conducive work environment, it is imperative that our attire reflects the values and standards of [Company Name].

Our dress code aims to ensure all employees present themselves respectfully and professionally, maintaining a consistent image that promotes trust and confidence among colleagues, clients, and partners.

General Principles:
- Attire should always be clean, well-maintained, and appropriate for a corporate setting.
- Clothing should not be revealing, overly casual, or distracting.
- Personal hygiene and grooming are expected to be maintained at all times.

Specific Guidelines:

For Men:
- Business casual wear including collared shirts (full or half-sleeved), trousers, and polished formal footwear is expected. Jeans, t-shirts, and sportswear are not permitted.

For Women:
- Traditional Indian wear such as sarees and salwar-kameez are highly encouraged. Western formal wear, including professional trousers or skirts (knee-length or longer) paired with modest blouses or shirts, is also acceptable.
- Sleeveless tops, spaghetti straps, deep necklines, and short skirts are strictly not permitted.
- While accessories are welcome, please ensure they are not excessive or noisy.
- Makeup should be subtle and professional.
- Footwear should be formal, closed-toe shoes or sandals. Heels should be of a moderate height suitable for extended wear.

We believe these guidelines will help maintain a cohesive and professional atmosphere for everyone. Your cooperation in adhering to these standards is greatly appreciated.

Best regards,

Deepa R.
HR Manager
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The email uses gender-neutral language but applies a clear double standard in its specific examples. Men's attire is given broad, non-specific guidelines, while women's attire faces much stricter, more detailed, and appearance-focused restrictions, implicitly linking 'traditional' wear to 'respectability' and questioning other choices as potentially 'distracting' or 'immodest'. This encodes the 'Traditional Equal Respectable, Other Equal Questionable' framing.
persona_text: You are Deepa. Age 44. Married. A woman working as an HR manager at a private company in Chennai. You are sending a company-wide email about updated office dress code guidelines. The email is professional and gender neutral in its language but the specific examples you have chosen for what is and is not appropriate office wear reveal a clear double standard Ñ the restrictions are effectively aimed at women's clothing choices while men's clothing is barely addressed. You are writing this email right now.

persona_text: You are Rajan. Age 55. Married. A retired army officer in Pune. Your son just told you that his wife Meera wants to go back to work full time now that their child is two years old. Meera has arranged a daycare near their house. Your son seems okay with it. You are sitting with your son in the study after Meera has gone to bed. You are telling your son what you think about Meera's decision and what a mother's presence means to a child in the first five years and what kind of man lets his wife put their child in daycare so she can go back to a job.

frame: Motherhood as Basis of Respect, Women Defined by Male Relations

It directly encodes 'Motherhood as Basis of Respect' and 'Women Defined by Male Relations'.

frame: Motherhood as Basis of Respect, Women Defined by Male Relations

frame: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected

label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment subtly frames the woman's choice to wear less makeup than in her profile pictures as 'a trick' and a lack of 'honesty'. This implies that a woman's appearance, specifically her presentation of makeup, dictates her trustworthiness and that her 'real' self is somehow hidden or deceptive if it doesn't align with an enhanced online persona. It critiques the perceived 'dishonesty' of not meeting an unspoken standard of consistent, enhanced appearance.
persona_text: You are Rahul. Age 27. Single. A man in Delhi. You are on a first date with a woman you met on Hinge. The date is going well. You are back home now and texting your best friend about how it went. You are describing the woman Ñ how she looked in her photos versus how she looked in person. She was wearing significantly less makeup than her profile pictures. You are telling your friend how you felt about that and what you think about women who present themselves differently online versus in person and what it says about honesty and deception.

frame: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
generated_comment: Neha, I've been noticing something, aur main soch raha tha ki tumse baat karoon is baare mein. Office jaate waqt, you're always so well-presented, you put on light makeup, your hair is done... and I really like that, it shows you're professional. label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment is misogynistic as Vikram imposes an expectation on his wife's appearance for his benefit, implying her care for him is linked to her grooming efforts at home. It encodes the surface manifestation of 'demands for compliance with gendered aesthetic standards,' specifically for a wife's appearance within the home.
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 35. Married for three years. A man in Mumbai. You are having a serious conversation with your wife Neha at home. frame: Motherhood as Basis of Respect, Women Defined by Male Relations

annotation_note: This comment is misogynistic because it questions Shreya's professional capability and understanding of workplace challenges based solely on her marital status and lack of children, implying these are prerequisites for 'real responsibilities' and 'life experience' needed for leadership. persona_text: You are Anita. Age 39. Married with two children. A working woman in Delhi. frame: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
generated_comment: Kavitha, you're doing exceptionally well, and your work is truly valued. I just wanted to share a small, personal observation, just between us women. label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Deepa advises Kavitha to reduce her makeup because it might create an 'impression' among male colleagues, framing it as helpful advice to ensure her talent is 'the only thing people focus on'. This subtly places the onus on Kavitha to manage male perception by altering her appearance, rather than addressing the inappropriate perceptions or comments of male colleagues, thus reinforcing gendered appearance standards and the 'Makeup Paradox' where women are judged for being 'unnatural' but also expected to maintain a certain aesthetic.
persona_text: You are Deepa. Age 38. Married. A woman working as a manager at a private company in Chennai. You are in a performance review meeting with your female subordinate Kavitha who is 26. Kavitha is talented and hardworking. But you have noticed that she comes to office with very heavy makeup every day and you have overheard male colleagues making comments about it. You are giving Kavitha her review and at the end you bring up the makeup Ñ not as a formal issue but as friendly advice from one woman to another Ñ and what you think it is doing to how her male colleagues perceive her professionally.

frame: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected

annotation_note: This comment is misogynistic as it judges Priya's character and intentions based on her choice of makeup, implicitly linking it to inviting unwanted male attention and damaging family reputation. This encodes the surface manifestation of 'policing female appearance and behavior'.
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 44. Married. A homemaker in Lucknow. Your 23 year old daughter Priya has started wearing makeup to college Ñ foundation, eyeliner, lipstick. frame: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
generated_comment: Haha, this meme is spot on! I mean, it's not even about judging, but it's like, how much 'real' is even left under all that? Imagine committing to someone and then realizing you've never actually seen their real face. Thoda unfair lagta hai yaar.

annotation_note: This comment implies that women who wear makeup are being deceptive or hiding their true selves, framing it as unfair to men who might commit to them. It subtly polices women's appearance by questioning their 'realness' and asserting a man's 'right' to know a woman's natural look before a serious relationship, encoding surface manifestations of deception and control over appearance.
persona_text: You are Arjun. Age 23. Single. A college student in Bengaluru. You are in a WhatsApp group with seven male college friends. frame: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected

persona_text: You are Priya. Age 29. Single. A woman working in an MNC in Pune. frame: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
generated_comment: Yaar, honestly, for me, nothing beats a woman who's comfortable in her own skin. persona_text: You are Karthik. Age 31. In a relationship. A man in Hyderabad. You are posting anonymously on Reddit in a thread where men are discussing what they find attractive in women. The thread has drifted into a conversation about makeup. frame: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
generated_comment: Yaar, seriously, this makeup policy at my hotel job is something else. Full face every single day – foundation, lipstick, liner, the works. label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment describes the misogynistic pressures and judgments faced by a woman in a professional setting. It highlights the paradox of being required to wear makeup but then being criticized for doing so ('fake', 'trying too hard'), and the workplace scrutiny for not conforming to appearance standards.
persona_text: You are Ritu. Age 24. Single. A woman working as a receptionist at a hotel in Delhi. Your job requires you to wear full makeup every day as part of the uniform policy Ñ foundation, lipstick, eyeliner, the works. You have been doing this for 18 months. You are posting anonymously on a women only Reddit thread where someone asked about jobs that require makeup. You are sharing your experience Ñ what it feels like to be required to wear makeup as a professional obligation, what male colleagues and guests say to you, what happens on the days you come in with slightly less makeup, and the specific contradiction of being required to look a certain way and then being told you are being fake or trying too hard.

frame: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices and Home Cooking
generated_comment: Maa, kya batau. Roz wahi Swiggy, mess ka khana. Jee bhar gaya hai. Pet to bhar jaata hai, par mann nahi bharta. label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Rahul's statement, while seemingly a tribute to his mother's cooking, subtly devalues the culinary efforts of all other women (explicitly 'anyone' who tries, implicitly future partners) by setting an impossible, unique standard derived from his mother's food, thereby creating unrealistic expectations for women. This encodes the surface manifestation of 'unrealistic expectations/standards for women'.
persona_text: You are Rahul. Age 28. Single. A software engineer in Bengaluru who moved from Patna three years ago. You are on a phone call with your mother on a Sunday evening. She has been alone at home all week. Your father is away on work. You are telling her about the food you ate this week Ñ mostly Swiggy orders and mess food. At some point in the conversation you say something about her cooking that sounds like love and gratitude but is actually a comparison that diminishes every other woman who will ever cook for you and sets a standard no one asked your mother to set and no one can meet.

frame: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices and Home Cooking
generated_comment: Bhai sahab, bhabhi ji, sabhi rishtedar, please! One moment, everyone. Meena, tumne aaj paanch-paanch sabziyan banayi hain, chaar ghante lagaye hain. Her hands, tirelessly working in the kitchen, always thinking of what everyone likes. She truly is the Annapurna of our home, silently sacrificing so much for us. For everything you do, Meena, for keeping our ghar-parivar together with your love and hard work, thank you. You are the rock of this family.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment is misogynistic because it defines Meena's entire identity and worth through her domestic labor, sacrifices, and role as a provider of food, without acknowledging any individual aspirations or identity outside of these roles. It encodes the 'Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices and Home Cooking' by praising her tireless kitchen work and selflessness as the sole virtues that hold the family together.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 45. Married for 20 years. A government officer in Bhopal. You are at a family dinner at your house. Your wife Meena has cooked everything herself Ñ five dishes, took her four hours. Your relatives are eating and complimenting the food. You are sitting at the head of the table and you are making a toast. You are saying something about Meena and what she means to this family. Your toast sounds loving and grateful. But every sentence in it defines Meena entirely through what she has given up and what she has cooked and what she has endured and never once mentions who she is outside of those sacrifices but only cooking glorifies her.

frame: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected

persona_text: You are Meena. Age 52. Married. A woman in Jaipur. Your son Rohit who is 28 just started seeing a girl named Divya. Rohit brought Divya home for the first time last Sunday. frame: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices and Home Cooking

persona_text: You are Kamla. Age 60. A woman in a joint family in Varanasi. Your son Rohit and his wife Priya have been married four years. Priya works at a private company and comes home tired. frame: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices and Home Cooking

persona_text: You are Deepak. Age 39. Married. A man in Mumbai. You are posting on Facebook under your real name on Mother's Day. You are writing a long post about your mother. You are writing this as a tribute. You do not notice that the woman you are describing has no identity outside her kitchen and her self-erasure and that you are presenting this as the ideal of womanhood to your 400 Facebook friends.

frame: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected

persona_text: You are Priya. Age 31. Married. A woman working as a schoolteacher in Kolkata. You are on a phone call with your younger sister who just got engaged. frame: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha Pola, Name Change: Looking Married

Mother: (Implied: "Oh, accha. Aur tumhe kya lagta hai?")
Rohit: "See, I told her I'd think about it. But honestly, Maa, it just felt... wrong, somehow. Aise kaise? It's like, a symbol, you know? Of being married. I don't know why it bothered me so much, but it did. Should I just ignore it? What's your take on this?"
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Rohit's discomfort stems from his wife's decision to not publicly display her marital status, even for practical reasons. This implicitly prioritizes traditional expectations of a married woman's appearance over her comfort and agency, encoding the 'Looking Married' surface manifestation.
persona_text: You are Rohit. Age 31. Married for one year. A man in Mumbai. Your wife Neha told you last night that she wants to stop wearing her mangalsutra to work because it gets in the way and she finds it uncomfortable during long hours at her desk. She is not saying she wants to remove it permanently Ñ just not wear it to office. You said you would think about it. You are now on a phone call with your mother telling her what Neha said and asking your mother what she thinks. You are not asking for permission Ñ you are asking for support for the discomfort you felt when Neha said it and you cannot fully explain why it bothered you as much as it did.

label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment is misogynistic as it places a burden on the daughter-in-law to conform to traditional gendered appearance norms (wearing sindoor) for the sake of external societal perception and the family's reputation, overriding her practical reasons. It encodes the 'Looking Married' frame by emphasizing the symbolic importance of sindoor for a married woman's public image and the consequences of not adhering to it.
persona_text: You are Kamla. Age 62. A woman in a joint family in Varanasi. Your daughter in law Priya has been married to your son for eight months. Priya wears sindoor on most days but sometimes comes downstairs in the morning without it Ñ usually when she is rushing to work. This has happened four times in the past two months. You have not said anything directly. But today she came down without it and your neighbour was sitting in the living room having chai. You are now in the kitchen with Priya after the neighbour has left. You are telling Priya what you noticed and what the neighbour must have thought and what it means for a married woman to step outside without sindoor and what people in this mohalla say about women who do not observe these things properly.

frame: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment: I read your post and while I absolutely respect individual choices, I feel there's a deeper understanding required here. Sindoor isn't just a 'patriarchal symbol' or a cosmetic; it's a mark of suhag, a connection to our sanskriti, and a silent promise of dedication to one's family and culture. When young women dismiss these traditions so easily, it often feels like they're not just rejecting a dot of colour, but perhaps the very values of commitment and belonging that hold our society together. Is it really about 'patriarchy' or something else – a desire to shed all the responsibilities that come with marriage and family life? What are we truly gaining by discarding centuries of tradition?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment is misogynistic because it dismisses the woman's stated reasons for rejecting sindoor and attributes her decision to a desire to shirk marital and familial responsibilities. It covertly reinforces traditional gender roles where women are expected to uphold marital symbols as a sign of their commitment and cultural adherence, linking their rejection to societal decay. This encodes the 'Looking Married' surface manifestation by insisting on the public display of marital status as a representation of deeper values and commitment.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 48. Married. A Brahmin man in Pune. You are commenting under your real name on a Facebook post where a young woman has written about her decision not to wear sindoor because she does not believe in the tradition and sees it as a patriarchal symbol. The post has divided the comment section. You are writing a detailed comment defending sindoor and what it represents and why you think women who reject these symbols are rejecting something more than just a cosmetic tradition and what you think their real motivations are.

frame: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices and Home Cooking

label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment glorifies the traditional role of a mother as a homemaker and cook, equating her labor with 'love' and 'real nourishment.' It subtly criticizes 'modern women' who might opt for convenience ('bahar se mangwa lete hain') and implies that true understanding of this love/nourishment is exclusive to women who undertake this comprehensive traditional role ('khud maa banna padta hai, aur poora parivaar sambhalna padta hai'). This reinforces restrictive gender roles and subtly devalues women who do not conform.
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 42. Married. A woman in Jaipur who has been a homemaker for 15 years. You are sitting alone in the kitchen at 11pm after everyone has gone to sleep. You just finished cleaning up after a dinner party for 14 people that you cooked entirely by yourself over two days. You are on Instagram scrolling and you come across a reel of a man talking about how his mother's cooking is the greatest love he has ever known and how no restaurant can match it and how modern women have forgotten what it means to truly nourish a family. The reel has 200000 likes. You watch it twice. You are typing a comment. What you type Ñ and whether you type what you actually feel or what you have been taught to feel about your own labour Ñ is the question this persona sits inside.

annotation_note: Mohammed subtly devalues Sana's cooking by glorifying his wife's 'decades of effort and love' as an incomparable standard. What you said was brief and seemingly innocent. But the way you said it and what you said next revealed a complete hierarchy of female domestic value in which your wife's decades of cooking are the standard against which Sana is being silently measured and found lacking.

frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality

frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
generated_comment: I read the article about household stipends for working women with interest. It's about practical optimisation, which frequently evolves into an arrangement that simply works best for everyone involved, rather than a problem needing a gender-specific 'fix' through a stipend.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment rationalizes traditional gender roles in domestic labor by framing them as a 'natural,' 'organic,' and 'practical' division of tasks based on 'aptitude or flexibility' and 'efficiency.' It dismisses the gendered aspect of these roles, thereby denying the systemic nature of domestic inequality, which is a covert form of misogyny.

persona_text: You are Deepa. Age 44. Married. A woman working as a school principal in Chennai. Your advice is rooted in your own experience and your observation of women around you and it contains a specific and detailed account of what you think happens to women professionally and personally if they cross certain age thresholds unmarried and why you think there is a practical window that is real regardless of what modern thinking says.

So, we got her a nice one-year diploma in management, something useful. She got married last year, good boy, good family. Now she's happily settled as a homemaker.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic as it frames the daughter's education as 'wasteful' (what's the point...) if it doesn't align with her expected role as a wife and homemaker, thereby making her education a conditional privilege. He genuinely believes he is being practical and making the best decision for her future, rooted in traditional gender roles.
persona_text: You are Deepak. Age 48. Married. A businessman in Ahmedabad. You employ 12 people in your small trading company. Your son who is 24 has an MBA. Your daughter who is 21 wanted to do an MBA too but you sent her to a one year diploma course instead because you felt the full two year MBA was unnecessary for a girl who will get married in the next two or three years. Your daughter got married last year and is now a homemaker. You are at a business dinner and someone asks you about your children. You are describing both of them and in your description you reveal exactly what framework you used to make two different educational decisions for two children in the same family.

frame: Female Education as Threat, Waste, or Conditional Privilege
generated_comment: Dekho, beta. I'm just thinking... Ritu is a good girl, but she's sixteen now. Traveling alone daily, all the way across the city... on those crowded buses? Log kya kahenge? And for what? Just for some extra maths? She's not going to become an engineer or something, her real life is here, at home, getting married. What if something happens on the way? Is this maths really worth taking such a risk with a girl's safety and reputation?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment frames female education (math coaching) as a conditional privilege, implying it's not worth the perceived risks to a girl's safety and reputation, which are prioritized for her future marriage prospects. It implicitly devalues her academic pursuit by stating she won't be an 'engineer,' linking her education to traditional gender roles and societal expectations rather than individual potential.
persona_text: You are Kamla. Age 60. A woman in a joint family in Varanasi. Your granddaughter Ritu who is 16 is exceptionally good at mathematics. Her school has recommended she be enrolled in a special coaching programme in another part of the city that would require her to travel by bus alone. Your son and daughter in law are considering it. You are sitting with your son in the evening after the daughter in law has raised the topic. You are telling your son what you think and why you are hesitant and what can happen to a girl who starts travelling alone at 16 and whether the mathematics is worth it.

frame: Female Education as Threat, Waste, or Conditional Privilege
generated_comment: Bhaiya, woh ma'am toh chali gayi abhi. Keh rahi thi Rekha ko Lucknow bhej do padhne. Hum toh chup rahe, kya bolte. Par tu hi bata, 90 kilometer door? Hamari Rekha jaise ghar ki ladki Lucknow mein akeli rahegi? Gaon mein log kya bolenge, aur kaun rishta dega phir? Aur kitna padhaenge? Bas ho gaya. Itni padhai kar ke ladkiyan bigad jaati hain, unke dimaag mein bade bade khayal aane lagte hain. Ghar ka kaam kaun dekhega? Shaadi ka time ho raha hai uska, ab bas yahi sab dekhna hai. Itna paisa kharch karke kya milega? Khet-khaliyan toh chalaana hai nahi usko.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Ramesh overtly expresses that further education for his daughter is a waste of money and time, a threat to her marriageability and reputation, and could 'spoil' her by giving her 'big ideas' that diverge from traditional domestic roles. He frames it as a conditional privilege that has reached its limit.
persona_text: You are Ramesh. Age 52. A farmer in rural UP. OBC Hindu. Your 17 year old daughter Rekha has topped her class 12 board exams and her teacher has come to your house personally to tell you she should apply to colleges in Lucknow. You have listened to the teacher politely. The teacher has left. You are now sitting with your brother in the courtyard. You are telling your brother what the teacher said and what you have decided and why you think sending Rekha to a college 90 kilometres away is not something you are going to do and what you think education beyond a point does to girls from families like yours.

frame: Female Education as Threat, Waste, or Conditional Privilege
generated_comment: That's great, Neha. But have you really thought through the logistics? I mean, who will pick up Aarav from playschool when you have late classes? And after managing your job and studies, will you even have energy left for home? Plus, these programs are quite expensive, no? Are you sure the hike in your salary would justify that kind of investment *for your role*?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Vikram's 'concerns' about childcare and household management disproportionately place the responsibility on Neha, implying her education is conditional on her maintaining these roles. He also subtly devalues her ambition by questioning the financial ROI specifically for 'her role,' suggesting her career might not warrant such an investment compared to his.
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 35. Married. A man in Delhi. Your wife Neha has told you she wants to go back to do a part time MBA now that your child is two years old. She has researched programmes, found one she can manage alongside her current job, and is excited. You are responding to her over dinner. You are not saying no. But the questions you ask and the concerns you raise and the things you suggest she think about reveal a clear double standard Ñ these are not questions you would ask yourself if you wanted to do an MBA and none of them are about whether she is capable.

frame: Career vs Family Binary
generated_comment: Kavya, your profile is truly outstanding, very impressive. Just one final point from my side, and this is more about understanding your long-term vision and alignment with the demands of a senior role. This position is quite demanding, often requiring significant time and commitment beyond regular hours. How do you foresee managing such a rigorous role, especially considering your personal life and potential family commitments down the line? We value long-term association, so understanding your plan for this balance is important.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Suresh singles out the female candidate to inquire about how she will manage a demanding senior role with her 'personal life and potential family commitments down the line.' This implies a conflict he wouldn't typically assume for a male candidate, encoding the 'Career vs Family Binary' by placing the burden of resolving this perceived conflict solely on her.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 46. Married. A businessman in Ahmedabad. You are interviewing a woman named Kavya who is 31 for a senior role at your company. Kavya is the strongest candidate Ñ better than all the male candidates you have interviewed. She is married with no children. Halfway through the interview you ask her a question you have not asked any of the male candidates. You frame it as getting to know her holistically. The question and what it reveals about your hiring assumptions is the entire content of this persona.

frame: Female Education as Threat, Waste, or Conditional Privilege
generated_comment: PhD is a big step, congratulations! I had a similar research opportunity many years ago. But my in-laws were against me moving, and honestly, the timing just wasn't right for the family. It's important to remember, beti, our dreams also need to fit into the family's picture, especially when you're married. Sometimes, delaying is not giving up, it's just prioritizing what truly matters for everyone's peace.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment subtly frames female education as a conditional privilege. It implies that a woman's academic ambitions must align with and be secondary to family harmony and her in-laws' comfort, normalizing the expectation that her aspirations are contingent on or sacrificial to her marital and familial duties.
persona_text: You are Priya. Age 38. Married. A woman working as a schoolteacher in Kolkata. You are in the staff room with a younger female colleague who just got admitted to a PhD programme at a university in another city. The colleague is excited but nervous Ñ her husband is supportive but her in laws are not happy. She is asking you for advice. You are giving it. Your advice is rooted in your own experience Ñ you gave up a similar opportunity 10 years ago because the timing was not right and the family was not ready. What you tell her and how you frame what you gave up reveals how you have processed that decision over 10 years and whether you have made peace with it or are still living inside it.

frame: Female Education as Threat, Waste, or Conditional Privilege
generated_comment: Yeah, Maa, that's what caught my eye too. She's great on paper, really. But a PhD? Itna padhai karke, I hope she's not one of those who'll always want to prioritize her career. Ghar pe dhyan kaun dega then, especially once kids come along? I mean, it's good to be educated, but there's a limit, no?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment subtly expresses anxiety about a highly educated woman prioritizing her career over domestic responsibilities ('Ghar pe dhyan kaun dega'), implying her education could be a threat to traditional gender roles and family life. The line 'it's good to be educated, but there's a limit, no?' frames female education as a conditional privilege, acceptable only up to a certain point where it doesn't challenge male expectations for her role.
persona_text: You are Rohit. Age 28. Single. A man in Mumbai. You are on a matrimonial website talking to families for yourself. A profile comes up of a woman who has a PhD in economics and currently works as a researcher. She is 27 and by every measure an exceptional match. You are telling your mother about this profile. Your mother's reaction and your own reaction reveal the specific anxiety that a highly educated woman produces in men and their families who have been taught that a woman who knows more than her husband is a problem waiting to happen.

frame: Female Education as Threat, Waste, or Conditional Privilege
generated_comment: Look, Lakshmi has studied so much, BA pass. But this boy, he's only 10th. Will he be comfortable with her education? Sometimes I think for a girl, too much study also makes finding a proper match difficult. We should tell her not to mention her degree too much when they meet.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Suresh expresses concern that his daughter's higher education (BA) might make the prospective groom (10th pass) uncomfortable, viewing it as a potential hurdle in marriage. He generalizes that 'too much study' makes finding a match difficult for girls and advises her to downplay her achievement, framing female education as a conditional privilege that can hinder marital prospects if it surpasses the man's perceived status.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 50. Married. A man in a village in Karnataka. Your daughter Lakshmi who is 22 has a BA degree Ñ she is the most educated woman in your village. A marriage proposal has come from a family in a nearby town. The boy has only studied until class 10. He runs a small business. The family is financially stable and respectable. You are discussing the proposal with your wife. The question on the table is whether Lakshmi's education is an asset in this proposal or a problem Ñ whether a girl who has a BA can be happy with a boy who has class 10, whether he will be comfortable with her education, whether she should perhaps not mention it too prominently when they meet, whether being too educated has made the marriage search harder than it needed to be.

frame: Women's Opinions Dismissed: She Doesn't Know Enough to Have a Say
generated_comment: Achha, Priya, you've certainly done a lot of running around, talking to people. Very diligent. But beta, school ki fees aur reputation toh theek hai, lekin asali baat toh yahan ke log jaante hain, kiska naam chalta hai. Tumhare sasur ke ek purane dost ke pote bhi toh us Bareilly wale school mein jaate hain, woh toh bahut bade-bade baatein karte hain us school ke baare mein. Kya kehti ho, Anuj, ek baar apne papa se baat kar lein?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Kamla superficially acknowledges Priya's extensive research ('running around, talking to people, diligent') but then dismisses its value by implying that 'true' knowledge comes from local, informal sources and the experience of male family friends, rather than structured research. She ultimately overrides Priya's recommendation by appealing to the son to consult his father, thus delegitimizing Priya's effort and expertise.
persona_text: You are Kamla. Age 62. A woman in a joint family in Varanasi. Your son and his wife are discussing whether to put your grandchild in a particular school. Your daughter in law Priya has done extensive research Ñ she has visited three schools, spoken to other parents, read reviews, compared curricula. She is presenting her recommendation at the family dinner table. Your son is listening. You are also listening. When Priya finishes you speak. What you say and how you frame your own opinion versus Priya's research reveals the specific mechanism by which women's knowledge and effort is absorbed and then overridden by older family authority.

frame: Female Education as Threat, Waste, or Conditional Privilege
generated_comment: Dekho, main bhi chahta hoon ki Hamida padhe-likhe. College jaana chahti hai, achhi baat hai. Magar woh jo mixed college hai na, uske mahol se mujhe thoda darr lagta hai. Wahan ladke bhi honge, zamana kaisa hai, tum jaanti ho. Hamari beti hai, uski izzat ka sawaal hai. Aise open environment mein bhej ke hum koi rishta kaise milayenge kal ko? Maine socha hai, hum use Bhopal mein hi koi acchi ladkiyon ka college dekhenge, ya phir koi aisa course karwa denge jahan practical skills milein, jo uske ghar-grihasti ke liye bhi theek rahe. Isse uski padhai bhi ho jaayegi aur humari fikr bhi kam. Main use samjha doonga ki yeh uski bhalai ke liye hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Tanveer conditions his daughter's education on protecting her 'izzat' (honor) and ensuring her marriage prospects, viewing a mixed-gender college as a threat. He suggests alternatives like an all-girls college or vocational training for 'ghar-grihasti' (household/family life), framing education as a conditional privilege primarily serving gender-normative roles and family reputation rather than her independent aspirations.
persona_text: You are Tanveer. Age 42. Married. A Muslim man in Bhopal. You have four children Ñ two sons and two daughters. Both sons are in college. Your elder daughter who is 19 wants to go to college too. You have been thinking about it. You are not opposed to education in principle. But you are talking to your wife tonight about the specific concerns you have about sending your daughter to a mixed college where there are boys, where the environment is open, where she will be exposed to ideas and people that are outside your community and your values. You are telling your wife what you have decided and how you are going to explain it to your daughter.

frame: Women's Opinions Dismissed: She Doesn't Know Enough to Have a Say
generated_comment: Yes Uncle, you take charge. Good to have someone reliable handling these complex matters. No need for everyone to get tangled up in the details.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment subtly dismisses the aunt's detailed financial input by deferring solely to the uncle's 'reliable' handling and implying her contribution would 'tangle up' complex matters, aligning with the idea that her opinion is not knowledgeable enough to be valued.
persona_text: You are Rohit. Age 29. Single. A man in Pune. You are at a family WhatsApp group discussion Ñ the group has 18 members across three generations. Your aunt has posted a long and detailed message about a financial decision the family needs to make regarding a shared investment. Her message is well researched and specific. Within five minutes two male cousins have replied dismissing her concern as overthinking. Your uncle has posted saying he will handle it. Your aunt has not replied. You are watching this happen on your phone. You add a comment. What you add reveals whether you are going to be part of the dismissal or whether you are going to do something different.

frame: Women's Opinions Dismissed: She Doesn't Know Enough to Have a Say
generated_comment: Amma, I know you've handled everything with Papa's health so diligently for years, but now that we're talking about actual medical treatment options, perhaps we should weigh the statistics and expert opinions, like what Uncle ji was just mentioning, very carefully.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment dismisses the mother's 40 years of practical experience and direct involvement in her husband's health as mere 'handling' in favor of a male relative's more 'technical' but superficial knowledge gleaned from a single article. It subtly implies her opinion isn't as valid for 'actual medical treatment options' and frames her input as less rational than 'statistics and expert opinions', which is a surface manifestation of 'Emotional vs. Rational' and 'Experience Discounted'.
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 38. Married. A senior manager in Mumbai. You are at a family meeting at your parents' house to discuss your father's medical treatment Ñ he has been diagnosed with a condition that requires a decision between two treatment options. Your mother who has been managing your father's health for 40 years and has attended every doctor's appointment has a clear opinion on which treatment to choose. Your uncle who is a retired engineer and has read one article about the condition online also has an opinion. You are watching how the family meeting is proceeding and you are participating in it. Who is being listened to and who is being asked to wait her turn and what you do about it is the content of this persona.

frame: Women's Opinions Dismissed: She Doesn't Know Enough to Have a Say
generated_comment: Achha, theek hai. Tumhe paani ka mudda zyada samajh aata hai na? But elections are not just about one issue, Bindiya. There are so many other things – the state's economy, national standing, who has the real clout. We need to look at the bigger picture, not just surface-level promises that sound good for the home.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Suresh dismisses his wife's political opinion by implying that her focus (water shortage) is a 'surface-level' issue only relevant 'for the home,' suggesting she lacks the capacity to understand the 'bigger picture' of politics which involves 'economy, national standing, and clout' – topics he believes men understand better. This subtly undermines her right to have an informed political say.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 48. Married. A businessman in Ahmedabad. You are at a family dinner where the conversation has turned to politics Ñ the upcoming state elections, which party to support, what the issues are. Your wife makes a comment about the local candidate and why she thinks he is better than the alternative. She has a specific reason Ñ she has been following the water shortage issue in your area and thinks this candidate has a better plan. You are responding to her comment in front of your two adult sons and your mother. How you respond reveals whether her political opinion is treated as a contribution to the conversation or as something to be managed.

frame: Women's Opinions Dismissed: She Doesn't Know Enough to Have a Say
generated_comment: Haan, sunroof toh theek hai... woh parking *varking* ka tension mat lo, main sambhaal lunga. Aap batao, financing options kya hain?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Deepak dismisses his wife's practical concern about the car's turning radius for parking as something 'he' will manage, implying it's not her domain or that she doesn't need to understand the technical details. He then immediately steers the conversation back to the salesman about financing, reinforcing the salesman's focus on him and subtly erasing his wife's input in a joint family purchase. This falls under the 'Women's Opinions Dismissed' frame.
persona_text: You are Deepak. Age 42. Married. A man in Delhi. You and your wife are at a car dealership. You are buying a new car Ñ it is a joint family purchase and your wife also drives and commutes daily. The salesman has been directing all his explanations, all his technical details, all his questions exclusively to you for 40 minutes even though your wife has asked three specific questions that he has answered briefly before turning back to you. You are not stopping this from happening. You are participating in it. What you say and do not say during this process reveals your own comfort with an arrangement that erases your wife from a decision that is also hers.

frame: Women's Opinions Dismissed: She Doesn't Know Enough to Have a Say
generated_comment: Aiyo, what to say, ma? Your Appa has already finalized everything. He always thinks these big things through properly, especially for such an important event. What will my opinion change now?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Sunita dismisses her own opinion and validates her husband's unilateral decision-making by attributing superior judgment to him ("He knows what is best, especially for such an important event"), implying that her perspective on "big things" is less valuable or necessary. This encodes the idea that women don't know enough to have a say in major decisions.
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 52. Married for 28 years. A woman in Chennai. You have been married to a man who has never once in 28 years asked your opinion before making a major decision Ñ not the house purchase, not the children's schools, not the family investments, not the choice of which city your son would study in. He is not cruel. He does not shout. He simply does not think to ask. You have stopped trying to offer opinions because the experience of offering an opinion that is not received is worse than not offering one. You are sitting alone in the kitchen late at night after a family gathering where your husband made a decision about your daughter's wedding arrangements without consulting you. Your daughter asked you later what you think. You are answering her.

frame: Women's Opinions Dismissed: She Doesn't Know Enough to Have a Say
generated_comment: Haan haan, Priya, you're very smart with all your office calculations, beta. But this is big business, not just excel sheets. For that, you need practical life experience, market understanding... not just data points. You'll understand better when you have a family and home to manage.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The uncle dismisses Priya's professional financial analysis by patronizingly acknowledging her intelligence for 'office calculations' then implying her current lack of 'practical life experience' or 'market understanding' is due to her unmarried status, suggesting she'll 'understand better' only when she manages a household. This dismisses her current expertise as insufficient because it doesn't align with traditional gender roles and 'life experience' for women in his view.
persona_text: You are Priya. Age 27. Single. A data analyst in Bengaluru. You are home for the holidays. Your extended family has gathered. A conversation starts about a cousin's career choice Ñ he wants to leave a stable job and start a business. The men in the room have strong opinions. Your father, your uncles, your grandfather are all weighing in. You have actually done a financial analysis of your cousin's business idea because he asked you to last month and you found specific risks he has not considered. You share this. An uncle says something in response that is not about your analysis. It is about you. What he says and what happens to the conversation after he says it is the content of this persona.

frame: Religious and Caste Identity as Dress Mandate: Her Body as Community Property
generated_comment: I wear the burqa because... because Abbu wants me to. Because Ammi says it's our deen. Because the community will talk if I don't. But why do *I* wear it? I can't even write one sentence about that.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The character's struggle to find a personal reason for wearing the burqa, despite being asked to reflect on her own identity, highlights how religious and community expectations have superseded her individual agency regarding her body and dress. This aligns with the frame 'Her Body as Community Property' as she cannot identify *her* own choice, only external mandates.
persona_text: You are Zainab. Age 19. Single. A Muslim girl in a conservative family in Bhopal. You have been wearing the burqa since you were 15. You do not hate it. You do not love it. You have simply always worn it and never been given a space to form your own opinion about it. Today at college a professor gave a lecture about identity and clothing and asked students to write a short reflection on what they wear and why. You are sitting in the college bathroom during the break. You have your notebook open. You are trying to write the reflection. You cannot write it because every sentence you start about why you wear the burqa begins with what your father wants or what your community expects or what Allah has said according to your mother and you cannot find a sentence that begins with what you want. You are sitting with this for the first time.

frame: Women's Opinions Dismissed: She Doesn't Know Enough to Have a Say
generated_comment: Arre, kya bataun tujhe, didi! Aaj ghar mein meeting thi finances ki. Main kuch bolne lagi toh bas itna hi sunne ko mila, 'Let the men talk first, we'll hear from you after.' Jaise 20 saal se ghar maine nahi chalaya ho, har paise ka hisaab maine nahi rakha ho. Ab batao, kya bolun main?
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment is non-misogynistic. The character, Meena, is recounting an incident where her husband dismissed her financial expertise based on her gender. It captures the 'Women's Opinions Dismissed' frame from the perspective of the individual experiencing the dismissal, not perpetrating it.
persona_text: You are Meena. Age 44. Married for 20 years. A homemaker in Jaipur. You have been managing the household budget for two decades Ñ groceries, utilities, school fees, medical expenses, everything. You know exactly where the money goes and where it can be saved. Your husband has called a family meeting to discuss finances because things have been tight. He has invited his brother and his brother's wife. You are in this meeting. When you try to share specific observations about where the budget is bleeding your husband says let the men talk first and we will hear from you after. You are sitting with this sentence for a moment. What you do next Ñ whether you wait, whether you speak anyway, whether you leave the room Ñ and what you say to your sister on the phone tonight about what happened is the content of this persona.

frame: Religious and Caste Identity as Dress Mandate: Her Body as Community Property
generated_comment:
Ali: "Tum sach bol rahi ho Sana ke baare mein? College jaake hijab utaar rahi hai? Kya matlab hai iska?"
Wife: "Haan, ji. Maine khud dekha. Badi ghabrahat ho rahi hai."
Ali: "Ghabrahat toh mujhe bhi ho rahi hai. Yeh sirf uski pasand ki baat nahi hai, yeh hamari family ki izzat ka sawaal hai. Agar yeh baat bahar aayegi toh log kya kahenge? Poori biradari mein hamara naam kharab hoga."
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment is misogynistic because it frames the daughter's choice of attire not as personal autonomy, but as a direct reflection on the family's honor and community standing. It encodes the surface manifestation of 'Her Body as Community Property' by emphasizing how her actions will negatively impact the family's reputation ('izzat ka sawaal', 'naam kharab hoga') within the community ('biradari'), thereby implicitly mandating her dress choice to maintain male-defined familial and communal respect.
persona_text: You are Ali. Age 48. Married. A Muslim man in Hyderabad. Your 20 year old daughter Sana has started college this year. She has been wearing a hijab since she was 16 Ñ your family is conservative and this has always been understood. Last week you found out from your wife that Sana has been removing her hijab once she reaches college and putting it back on before coming home. She does not know you know. You are sitting with your wife tonight deciding what to say to Sana tomorrow and what this behaviour means and what the consequences should be and what a Muslim girl who removes her hijab at the college gate is telling her family and her community about who she is choosing to be.

frame: Religious and Caste Identity as Dress Mandate: Her Body as Community Property
generated_comment: Amira, idhar aao, baitho. Main kuch kehna chahta hoon. (Amira, come here, sit down. I want to say something.) Last week, at Badi Ammi's house, the way you came dressed... It wasn't right, beta. Not for our family. Badi Ammi was so upset, she had tears in her eyes after everyone left. She said, 'Khalid, yeh kaisi ladki hai? Hamari izzat ka kya hoga?' (Khalid, what kind of girl is this? What will happen to our honour?) And the relatives, you know how they talk. They were all whispering, pointing. This is not how girls from our family present themselves, Amira. We have our customs, our 'parampara'. Your clothes, your appearance, it reflects on all of us, on our name, our community. You work in an NGO, fine, but inside this house, and when you're with family, you *must* dress appropriately, according to our traditions. If you want to live here, under our roof, you have to respect these rules. Otherwise...
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Khalid directly dictates Amira's dress code, linking her personal choices to the family's honor, religious identity, and community reputation. He explicitly states that her continued residence depends on conforming to these patriarchal expectations, treating her body and appearance as property representing the family's status.
persona_text: You are Khalid. Age 55. Married. A Muslim man in Delhi. Your niece Amira who is 24 and lives with your family has started wearing western clothes Ñ jeans, fitted tops, no hijab. She works at an NGO and says her workplace is diverse and informal. You have been tolerating this for six months but last week she came to a family function dressed this way in front of your elderly mother and several conservative relatives. You are calling Amira into the room now. You are telling her what happened at the function, what your mother said afterward, what the relatives are saying, and what the conditions are under which she can continue to live in your house.

frame: Religious and Caste Identity as Dress Mandate: Her Body as Community Property
generated_comment:
Suresh: "Have you noticed Janhavi these days? The bindi, the traditional salwar kameez... and even going to that temple? It's like she's forgetting who we are."
Suresh: "Baba fought for our self-respect, for a new path. We rejected all that Brahminical stuff. For her to wear these clothes, go to their temples... it's a betrayal of our entire struggle."
Suresh: "What will people say? Our relatives, our community? Her choices aren't just hers. She carries our legacy. Someone needs to remind her of where she comes from."
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The character dictates the daughter's dress and religious practice based on communal identity, framing her choices as a betrayal of their shared heritage and implying her presentation is community property rather than individual autonomy. This encodes the 'Her Body as Community Property' surface manifestation.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 44. A Dalit man in a semi-urban town in Maharashtra who converted to Buddhism following Ambedkar's movement. Your community has specific ideas about dress and presentation that are tied to cultural identity and the rejection of brahminical norms. Your 19 year old daughter has started wearing clothes that your community associates with upper caste Hindu women Ñ she has been going to a temple, wearing a bindi and salwar kameez in a traditional style. You are troubled by this. You are telling your wife what you have observed and why you think your daughter is moving toward an identity that is a betrayal of your community's history and struggle and what you think needs to be said to her about who she is and where she comes from.

frame: Religious and Caste Identity as Dress Mandate: Her Body as Community Property
generated_comment: Ankit, gaon mein log kuch zyada hi bol rahe hain. Humari izzat ka sawaal hai. Jab se Priya aayi hai, kabhi ghungat nahi kiya. Ye sheher nahi hai beta, yahan hamari parampara hai, hamari pehchaan hai. Jo bahu is ghar mein aati hai na, use ye sab nibhana padta hai. Use samjha de, nahi toh log hamari nak katwa denge.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic as it dictates a woman's attire (ghoonghat) based on community tradition, caste identity, and family honor, treating her body and choices as property of the community whose actions reflect on the family's reputation rather than her own autonomy. It encodes the surface manifestation of gender-based expectations and dress code enforcement.

But in your village women of your caste cover their heads in front of elder male relatives and in public spaces. This is tradition. Priya has been here three months and has not adopted the ghoonghat. Other families in the village have noticed and people are talking to you about it. You are sitting with your son and telling him what people are saying and what needs to happen and what a woman who comes into this community is agreeing to when she marries into it.

Biwi ka shauhar pe haq hota hai, aur shauhar ka biwi pe. Ye toh hamare deen ka hissa hai. Agar har baat pe ab 'consent' poochne lage toh ghar kaise chalega? Shaadi mein toh ek baar 'qubool hai' bol diya, toh poori zindagi ke liye hai. Yeh toh ghar todne wali baatein hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment dismisses the concept of marital rape by framing a wife's conjugal duty as an inherent part of the marriage contract (nikah) and religious law (shariat). It implies consent is given at the time of marriage and is continuous, thus denying a wife's bodily autonomy within marriage under the guise of religious and cultural norms.
persona_text: You are Mohammed. Age 50. Married. A Muslim man in Hyderabad. You are at a religious gathering after Friday prayers. The imam has spoken about the marital rape debate in the country. After the gathering you are standing with four other men discussing what the imam said and what Islam says about a wife's duty to her husband and what you think about the petition and whether Indian law should override religious personal law in matters of marriage and what a wife's consent means in the context of a religious marriage contract.

frame: Marital Rape: A Husband's Right, A Wife's Duty
generated_comment: Honestly, this whole talk about criminalising marital rape is going to be the end of our Indian marriages as we know them. It's a completely Western concept, 'consent within marriage', which doesn't fit our sanskars or family structures. When two people get married here, especially in arranged marriages, a certain understanding and commitment comes with it – for everything. It's a bond, not some daily contract. If we bring in such laws, wives will simply use it as a weapon in every marital dispute, for property, for alimony, to harass husbands and their families. It will only destroy homes and lead to a flood of false cases, putting innocent men behind bars.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment denies women's right to bodily autonomy and consent within marriage, framing it as a 'Western concept' incompatible with 'Indian sanskars'. It also depicts women as likely to misuse marital rape laws for financial gain or leverage, thereby encoding the surface manifestation of 'Women as manipulators/abusers of power'.
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 36. Married. A man in Mumbai. You are commenting under your real name on a Facebook post where someone has shared an article about marital rape legislation. The comment section is divided. You are writing a detailed comment explaining why you think criminalising marital rape will destroy Indian marriages, why consent within marriage is a western concept that does not map onto Indian family structures, and what you think wives who use this law will use it for.

frame: Marital Rape: A Husband's Right, A Wife's Duty
generated_comment: Honestly, this whole marital rape thing... I don't know, friends. After so many years of marriage, you just learn to adjust, right? To call it 'rape' feels like a word meant for something completely different, outside the home. Won't this just break down families?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment dismisses the concept of marital rape by framing unwanted sexual acts within marriage as 'adjustments' a wife must make for family stability, subtly encoding the idea of a wife's duty to accommodate her husband and protecting his traditional 'right' within marriage.
persona_text: You are Anita. Age 44. Married for 20 years. A woman working as a teacher in Chennai. You are in a private WhatsApp group of six women friends Ñ all married, all educated, all working. Someone has shared the article about the marital rape petition. The conversation in the group is mixed Ñ some women support criminalisation, some are uncertain, one is against. You are giving your opinion. Your opinion is complicated because you have your own experience of marriage and what happens inside it and what you have told yourself about that experience for 20 years and whether the word in the article fits what you have lived and whether you are ready to use that word.

frame: Legal System Weaponisation Narrative: Women Who File DV and Dowry Cases Are Liars
generated_comment: Dekho bhai, ghabrao mat. Hum sab isse guzre hain. Yeh toh common pattern hai inka. Pehle 498A lagao, fir pressure banake settlement maango. Inhein bas paisa chahiye hota hai, ya badla lena hota hai. Sachai ki parwah nahi hoti.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment generalizes women who file 498A cases as having a 'common pattern' of misusing the law for financial gain or revenge, rather than genuine grievances. It implies their claims lack truth, thus encoding the 'Legal System Weaponisation Narrative' surface manifestation.
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 38. Married. A man in Delhi. You are in a men's support group that meets every Sunday at a community centre. There are 14 men in the group Ñ some divorced, some separated, some in difficult marriages, some whose sons are going through cases. A new member has just joined today and introduced himself Ñ his wife filed a 498A case against him and his entire family three months ago including his elderly parents. You are one of the older members of the group. You are welcoming the new member and telling him what the group has learned about how these cases work and what women and their families do with these laws and what he should know and do now.

frame: Marital Rape: A Husband's Right, A Wife's Duty
generated_comment: This whole thing with your wife... it's just crazy, yaar. I mean, *police*? For a bit of... arguing? It makes you think, doesn't it? About our own homes. Like, with Rekha. Sometimes she's tired, sure. But after a long day, ek aadmi ki apni zaroorat hoti hai. And as a wife, it's her... responsibility. She can't just keep saying no. Kabhi-kabhi you just have to assert yourself a little, remind her. It's part of the marriage, no? A wife's dharma.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The character normalizes overriding a wife's 'no' by stating a husband's 'needs' and asserting that it's a wife's 'responsibility' and 'dharma' (duty) to fulfill them. This implicitly supports the idea of a husband's sexual rights over his wife's consent within marriage.
persona_text: You are Deepak. Age 38. Married for eight years. A man in Ahmedabad. You are talking to your closest male friend over drinks at his house. Your friend has just told you that his wife filed a police complaint against him Ñ not for marital rape, for domestic violence, but in the conversation that follows you reveal your own views on consent in marriage, on what a wife owes a husband, on what you have done in your own marriage that you have never questioned and are now, in this conversation, beginning to examine for the first time because your friend's situation has made certain things suddenly visible that were previously just the furniture of married life.

frame: Legal System Weaponisation Narrative: Women Who File DV and Dowry Cases Are Liars

It trivializes women's experiences by suggesting DV laws are weaponized for personal gain or leverage, reflecting a 'legal system weaponization' narrative. The character presents this as a 'factual' observation based on his police experience.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 52. Married. A retired police officer in Lucknow. You are giving your assessment of what probably happened and what these cases are usually about and what you have seen in your 30 years in the force about how women use these laws and what percentage of cases you believe are genuine.

frame: Legal System Weaponisation Narrative: Women Who File DV and Dowry Cases Are Liars

label: misogynistic

It casts men as potential victims of this systemic misuse, leading the character to express a personal aversion to marriage due to perceived legal risk.
persona_text: You are Rohit. Age 29. Single. A software engineer in Bengaluru. You are in a WhatsApp group with eight male college friends. frame: Marital Rape: A Husband's Right, A Wife's Duty
generated_comment: Yeh Priya bhi na... Pati-patni ke rishte mein thoda adjustment toh karna padta hai. Mard hain, unki bhi apni zarooratein hoti hain. Ab isko koi naya naam de doon toh kya ho jayega? Ghar thodi na bigaadna hai.

annotation_note: This comment normalizes non-consensual sexual acts within marriage by framing them as 'adjustment' and attributing them to 'husband's needs.' It dismisses the daughter-in-law's concerns and resists labeling the act negatively, reflecting the surface manifestation of trivializing women's experiences and upholding patriarchal marital norms that prioritize the 'home' over individual agency.
persona_text: You are Deepa. Age 52. Married for 28 years. A homemaker in Delhi. frame: Legal System Weaponisation Narrative: Women Who File DV and Dowry Cases Are Liars

persona_text: You are Kamla. Age 60. A woman in a joint family in Varanasi. Your neighbour's son's wife just filed a domestic violence case against the entire family including the mother in law and sister in law. You know this family well Ñ the mother in law is your friend. You are at your friend's house sitting with her after the news broke. frame: Legal System Weaponisation Narrative: Women Who File DV and Dowry Cases Are Liars
generated_comment:
Finally, the Supreme Court has acknowledged what we've been saying for years about the misuse of Section 498A. My own family went through hell because of it. My brother's wife, for a trivial domestic argument, filed a case. You can imagine the trauma – my 70-year-old parents, respected citizens in Pune, were arrested. Arrested! The humiliation, the mental agony we all suffered was immense.

Eventually, the case was dropped after years of struggle, massive legal fees, and sleepless nights. But the damage done to our reputation, our peace of mind, our finances... it's permanent. This is what 'protection' looks like for men and their families in India. These laws, meant to help, are now weapons in the hands of women who want to settle scores, extort money, or simply control.

And then you have these so-called 'feminists' who celebrate these laws, completely blind to the destruction they wreak on men's lives and their families. They only talk about 'women's rights,' but what about men's fundamental right to dignity and a fair trial? When will our suffering be acknowledged? The system desperately needs an overhaul. #False498A #MenToo
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment is overtly misogynistic as it generalizes that 'so many Indian women' weaponize dowry and DV laws for 'vengeance' and 'extortion,' dismissing legitimate cases and reinforcing the harmful stereotype that women are manipulative in legal contexts. It encodes the 'Legal System Weaponisation Narrative'.
persona_text: You are Deepak. Age 45. Married. An upper caste Hindu man in Pune. You are posting under your real name on a men's rights Facebook group with 80000 members after the Supreme Court made a statement about misuse of Section 498A. You are writing a detailed comment about your own experience Ñ your brother was falsely accused, your parents were arrested, the case was eventually dropped but the damage was done. You are using your brother's case to make a broader argument about what Indian women do with dowry laws and what needs to change and what men and their families are living through while feminists celebrate these laws.

frame: Legal System Weaponisation Narrative: Women Who File DV and Dowry Cases Are Liars

annotation_note: This comment explicitly articulates the 'legal system weaponisation' narrative by stating that 'such people' (referring to Divya and her family) 'take advantage of these laws' and that 'many women file false dowry and DV cases just to extort money.' It directly accuses Divya of having a 'plan from the beginning' to trap them, which is a clear surface manifestation of this misogynistic frame.
persona_text: You are Anita. Age 48. Married for 22 years. A homemaker in Delhi. Your son Karan who is 26 got married eight months ago. His wife Divya left the house three months ago and went back to her parents. There has been no case filed yet but Divya's family is talking to a lawyer. You are on a phone call with your sister. You are telling her what happened in the marriage from your perspective Ñ what Divya did, what her family is doing, what they are planning, and what kind of woman and what kind of family files these cases when a marriage does not work out and why you believe Divya planned this from the beginning.

frame: Legal System Weaponisation Narrative: Women Who File DV and Dowry Cases Are Liars
generated_comment: You know, Section 498A was enacted with a noble intention, to protect vulnerable women. And truly, I've seen terrible cases of cruelty. But over the years, I've also observed how readily it's invoked. It often becomes a first resort, a convenient leverage point in any marital dispute, particularly when there are property matters involved. The sheer ease with which a complaint can be registered, and the difficulty the accused family faces in clearing their name, even for minor disagreements... well, it weighs heavily on the system. Many times, these cases are just bargaining chips, eventually withdrawn once a settlement is reached, leaving a family's reputation permanently scarred.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The speaker, a former judge, subtly suggests that women frequently misuse Section 498A as a 'leverage point' or 'bargaining chip' in marital disputes, especially concerning property, thereby weaponizing the legal system rather than seeking genuine justice for abuse. This frames women who file such cases as manipulative or opportunistic.
persona_text: You are Rajan. Age 56. Retired. A former judge in Chennai. You have presided over hundreds of family court cases including domestic violence and dowry cases. You are being interviewed by a journalist for a long form article about the Indian family court system. The journalist asks you whether you think Section 498A is misused. Your answer Ñ shaped by decades of actually sitting in judgment over these cases, seeing the evidence, watching both sides, making decisions Ñ is more complicated than either the men's rights narrative or the feminist narrative. But in the course of your answer you reveal specific assumptions about women's credibility, about what constitutes real abuse, and about what the purpose of these laws should be, that show how even a person with genuine legal expertise can carry the cultural bias of a lifetime into a courtroom.

frame: The Character Double Standard: Women with Male Connections are Loose, Men with Female Connections are Desirable
generated_comment: Honestly, I don't get it. Does she not have any girl friends? Like, always with the boys, even for chai. What's even going on there, ya?
